title: Fundamentals of Digital Transformation
description: This course introduces students to the concept of digital transformation and the Fourth Industrial Revolution, and their various applications in different fields of life and work. It establishes the foundation for understanding the adoption of the latest communications and information technologies in developing digital infrastructure and facilitating the transition towards the digital future, including smart cities, smart transportation, and IoT applications.
learning_outcomes: Students will recognize the concept of digital transformation. Students will understand the evolution of digital transformation. Students will explain the role of communication technologies in digital transformation. Students will acquire skills to identify digital transformation opportunities. Students will discover optimal solutions for digital transformation challenges. Students will collaborate in groups and work as a team on digital transformation projects.

title: Introduction to Computing and Algorithms
description: This course provides students with a breadth-first overview of essential concepts and principles of various computing fields. Topics covered include history of computing, binary number system and data representation, computer architecture and components, basics of problem solving and algorithms, basics of programming languages, operating systems, artificial intelligence, network technology, database technology, computer security, and the role of computers in society. The lab covers Python basics including variables, conditionals, loops, functions, arrays, data structures, algorithms, SQL, and HTML.
learning_outcomes: Students will realize the important history of computing and its developments. Students will define operating systems their types roles and functionalities. Students will illustrate computer architecture its main components and their functionalities. Students will describe the role of general information and database systems and artificial intelligence. Students will apply knowledge of programming algorithms data structures SQL and HTML to solve real life problems. Students will develop computational solutions for simple problems using appropriate algorithms. Students will code programs that utilize arrays. Students will apply knowledge of networking and security to solve real life problems. Students will demonstrate social and ethical issues in technological disciplines such as privacy liability copyrights and social awareness.

title: Discrete Structures
description: This course provides an overview of Discrete Structures for Computing. Topics covered include basic logic, sequences, mathematical induction, recursion and recurrence relations, sets, relations and functions, elementary number theory and methods of proof, basics of counting, graphs and trees. The course develops mathematical thinking skills essential for computer science.
learning_outcomes: Students will recognize basic logic and truth tables. Students will define different strategies of mathematical proof. Students will describe mathematical concepts including sets relations functions graphs and trees. Students will justify the truth of propositions and prepare mathematical proofs. Students will differentiate between sets relations and functions. Students will calculate matrix addition and multiplication and evaluate basic counting problems. Students will work both independently and collaboratively on mathematical problems.

title: Programming 2
description: This course introduces the concepts and principles of object-oriented programming (OOP) using Java. Students learn to design, implement, and maintain software systems using OOP techniques including class design, object instantiation, and method implementation. Topics covered include encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, abstraction, exception handling, text file I/O, abstract classes and interfaces, generics, and built-in classes including GUI components, data structures, and event-driven programming.
learning_outcomes: Students will recall OOP principles and their benefits and uses. Students will write programs that define classes and use objects. Students will design classes following principles of abstraction encapsulation and information hiding. Students will write programs utilizing inheritance and polymorphism. Students will apply exception handling techniques. Students will write programs that read write and modify text files. Students will demonstrate understanding of abstract classes and interfaces. Students will use generic classes and interfaces. Students will code programs using the Java API including GUI data structures and event-driven programming.

title: Probability
description: This course introduces mathematical statistics and probability theory. Topics include fundamental probability concepts, classical definitions of probability, conditional probability, independent and disjoint events, Bayes theorem, random variables, discrete and continuous probability distributions, joint probability distributions, mathematical expectation, variance and covariance, Chebyshev's theorem, discrete distributions including binomial multinomial hypergeometric negative binomial and Poisson, continuous distributions including uniform and normal distributions, and functions of random variables including transformations and moment generating functions.
learning_outcomes: Students will define discrete probability distributions and fundamental probability concepts including random variables and moments. Students will explain the difference between discrete and continuous probability distributions. Students will use theorems to find statistical measures including expected value variance moment generating functions correlation and covariance. Students will apply mathematical rules to find expected values variances and moment generating functions of random variables. Students will evaluate mean vectors covariance matrices and correlations of random variables. Students will find the relationship between probability density functions and cumulative distribution functions. Students will work effectively in groups and take responsibility for their own learning and professional development.

title: Linear Algebra
description: This course introduces students to the fundamental concepts of linear algebra culminating in abstract vector spaces and linear transformations. Topics include matrix algebra and determinants, systems of linear equations, inverse of a matrix and LU factorization, vector spaces and subspaces, orthogonality, linear transformations, and diagonalization including eigenvalues and eigenvectors. The subject is important for computer scientists in fields of signal and image processing, computer graphics, robotics, and information security. MATLAB is used as a programming environment.
learning_outcomes: Students will define operations on matrices and their properties. Students will define the concepts of vector spaces and linear transformations. Students will solve systems of linear equations. Students will calculate eigenvalues and eigenvectors for matrix diagonalization. Students will model mathematical problems in a matrix environment. Students will apply linear algebra concepts to computer science applications including signal processing image processing and computer graphics. Students will communicate concepts and techniques in oral presentations and interact in groups collaboratively.

title: Digital Logic Design
description: This is an introductory level course that gives its participants ability to analyze and design digital circuits. Students learn procedural approaches to designing digital circuits starting from specification of the problem. Students become familiar with the number systems that are used in computers and other digital circuits. They learn to use Boolean algebra and logic gates. Methods of manipulating and simplifying Boolean expressions are learned. Basic combinational logic function models are designed. Students become familiar with arithmetic functional blocks, latches, flip-flops, counters, and registers. Sequential circuits are also designed, and students are introduced to PLD programming. In addition to the classroom portion of the course, there are several laboratory sessions where students build and test their logic designs.
learning_outcomes: Students will recognize fundamental principles of designing digital circuits. Students will describe combinational and sequential circuits. Students will minimize combinational logic circuits. Students will analyze combinational and sequential logic circuits. Students will design combinational and sequential logic circuits. Students will implement logic circuits using PLDs. Students will work both independently and collaboratively. Students will communicate concepts and techniques in oral presentations.

title: Technical Writing
description: This course is designed to equip bachelor degree students with the necessary skills to communicate effectively in technical writing. Students will learn the principles and techniques of technical writing, which includes writing reports, memos, manuals, and other technical documents.
learning_outcomes: Students will understand the principles and techniques of technical writing including audience analysis, purpose and scope, organization, formatting, and research. Students will identify and analyze different types of technical documents including reports, proposals, manuals, and instructions. Students will understand the importance of grammar, punctuation, and style in technical writing. Students will write clear, concise, and effective technical documents for a variety of audiences and purposes. Students will use visual aids, formatting, and other tools to enhance the clarity and impact of technical documents. Students will conduct research and analyze data to produce technical documents that are informative and useful to the intended audience. Students will understand the importance of ethical and professional conduct in technical writing including attribution, plagiarism, and confidentiality. Students will demonstrate autonomy and independence in their writing while also seeking feedback and guidance when necessary.

title: Computer Network 1
description: This course provides students with fundamentals of computer networking, the OSI model, and the TCP/IP model. A discussion of the physical layer of the Internet model and the transmission media is given. Students will learn switching techniques which can be used in several layers. A discussion of the data-link layer and network layer of the Internet model will also be given. The lab gives students practical experiments on computer networks including an introduction to Packet Tracer network simulator, configuring initial switch settings, building an Ethernet, and configuring IPv4 Protocol.
learning_outcomes: Students will describe conceptual models of networking including TCP/IP and OSI models. Students will describe the characteristics of the transmission media and transmission impairments. Students will explain the fundamentals of data and signals. Students will explain the operation and principles of multiplexing and switching techniques. Students will analyze the principles and operations of the Data-Link Layer protocols. Students will analyze the principles and operations of Network Layer and Internet protocols. Students will discuss concepts and techniques during class. Students will participate in groups collaboratively.

title: Database 1
description: This course covers concepts and techniques used in constructing relational databases. Students learn the rules of modelization and normalization of databases, the implementation and use of the SQL DDL language. Through relational algebra, they learn to solve simple and complex queries and translate them into SQL DML. They also acquire how to convert the Entity Relation Model to a Relational Model. The lab gives students practical experiments on Oracle DBMS, including building databases using SQL, inserting, deleting, and updating rows and tables, and writing simple and complex queries such as subqueries, joins, group by, exist, and negation forms.
learning_outcomes: Students will describe the concepts of database. Students will explain the querying concept. Students will explain the relational model. Students will demonstrate understanding of SQL language including SQL DDL and SQL DML simple queries. Students will use advanced SQL querying with complex SQL DML queries. Students will demonstrate understanding of the entity-relationship model design and its mapping to the relational database model. Students will communicate concepts and techniques in oral presentations. Students will work both independently and collaboratively.

title: Computer Organization and Architecture
description: This course provides students with basic knowledge in fundamentals of computer design, performance evaluation, instruction set principles, processor organization and design, pipelining, instruction and arithmetic pipelines, dynamic and speculative execution, precise exception, CISC, RISC, and VLIW processors, memory hierarchy, virtual memory, multilevel caches, storage and I/O, introduction to multi-core, multiprocessors, and clusters, and new trends in computer architecture.
learning_outcomes: Students will describe the evolution of computer architecture. Students will recognize computer components and their interconnections. Students will review the logic and number system behind the computer revolution as well as instruction sets and their relevance to computing. Students will explore the fundamentals of data communication systems. Students will test the different data transmission techniques. Students will work both independently and collaboratively. Students will communicate concepts and techniques in oral presentations.

title: Data Structures
description: This course is designed to provide students with an overview of data structures and types that are a prerequisite for understanding algorithms. Topics include lists, stacks, queues, recursion, trees, sets and maps, sorting, graphs, and complexity. Laboratory exercises demonstrate problem-solving techniques using Java, covering the implementation of basic data structures including lists, stacks, queues, trees, and heaps, as well as the computational complexity of searching and sorting algorithms.
learning_outcomes: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the basics of data structures and fundamentals of OOP. Students will recognize the difference between linked list and array implementation. Students will outline the basic concepts of data structures including linked lists, stacks, queues, trees, and graphs. Students will apply searching and sorting algorithms using several data structures. Students will make use of special algorithms like recursion for data structures manipulation. Students will develop and solve problems and find algorithms complexity for several data structures. Students will identify theoretically and practically the strength of each searching and sorting algorithm using several data structures. Students will appreciate learning as a lifelong process and the importance of data structure and algorithm development. Students will interact in groups collaboratively. Students will communicate concepts and techniques in oral presentations.

title: Web Technologies 1
description: This course covers concepts and techniques used in constructing interactive web applications. Topics include browser-side web facilities such as HTML5, cascading stylesheets, JavaScript, and the document object model. Students learn to create dynamic and interactive websites using advanced HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. The lab gives students practical experiments on web page development including building static websites using HTML5, cascading style sheets, JavaScript, and HTML forms.
learning_outcomes: Students will describe the web site creation process. Students will define navigation of web sites. Students will explain the layout of web sites. Students will develop web pages using HTML and HTML5. Students will use Cascading Style Sheets including CSS and CSS3. Students will develop web applications using client-side programming with JavaScript and an introduction to jQuery including animation, scaling, and menus. Students will work both independently and collaboratively. Students will practice concepts and techniques in oral presentations.

title: Software Engineering 1
description: This course introduces students to object-oriented system design and development. The OOD process uses the Unified Modeling Language (UML) to design system architecture. Students learn use cases, class diagrams, sequence diagrams, and other analysis artifacts as input from the OOA phase, which are then used in OOD to identify, define, and design system classes and objects, as well as their relationships, interfaces, and implementations.
learning_outcomes: Students will describe the object-oriented concepts. Students will list UML diagrams that illustrate the structure and dynamic behavior of a system including class diagrams, sequence diagrams, activity diagrams, and state diagrams. Students will describe UML Use Case diagram notation and style to depict high-level user requirements and write Use Case Centric Functional Requirements Specifications. Students will recognize the design theory of class diagrams, sequence diagrams, activity diagrams, and state diagrams. Students will demonstrate the fundamentals of object orientation. Students will recognize the basic concepts, principles, and theories relating to object orientation. Students will develop and explain the design of different UML diagrams using case studies. Students will work both independently and collaboratively. Students will interact in concepts and techniques in oral presentations.

title: Computer Networks 2
description: This course is an extension of CS1006 - Computer Networks 1, focusing on the Network Layer and the Link Layer of the TCP/IP model. Network Layer topics include the Internet Protocol (IP), generalized forwarding and SDN, middleboxes, routing algorithms, intra-AS routing, routing among ISPs, the SDN control plane, ICMP, and network management using SNMP and NETCONF/YANG. Link Layer topics include error detection and correction, multiple access links and protocols, switched local area networks, link virtualization, data center networking, and the lifecycle of a web page request. Lab sessions use Wireshark to analyze IP, NAT, ICMP, Ethernet, ARP, and WiFi protocols.
learning_outcomes: Students will describe the architecture and operation of the Network Layer and the Link Layer including the different protocols used at each layer and how they interact. Students will explain the different routing algorithms used in the Internet and how they are used to route packets from source to destination. Students will apply their knowledge of network architecture, protocols, routing, and security to analyze and optimize network performance. Students will configure and manage complex networks using industry-standard protocols and tools such as OSPF, BGP, and SNMP. Students will analyze and optimize network performance using tools and techniques such as packet sniffers, network monitoring systems, and traffic shaping. Students will communicate effectively with other network administrators and users about network issues and solutions.

title: Web Technologies 2
description: This course introduces the latest trends and best programming practices with PHP and the skills needed for practical web programming applications. Students learn to use MySQL with PHP to build database-driven websites, master SQL and database design principles, and create three-tiered data applications such as sites requiring user login/authentication, automated web content, interactive websites, and shopping carts. The lab covers building dynamic websites, web server configuration, MySQL, and server-side PHP programming including connecting PHP with MySQL.
learning_outcomes: Students will demonstrate understanding of the TCP/IP models and protocols with emphasis on the application and transport layers. Students will describe web services and request/response message format, caching, and cookies concepts. Students will describe the PHP language and web server configurations. Students will define functions and control structures of PHP language. Students will explain PHP data types and operators. Students will apply MySQL database with PHP. Students will initiate groups collaboratively. Students will plan concepts and techniques in oral presentations.

title: Operating Systems
description: This is the first course in operating systems theory and design. Students understand the core concepts of operating systems, such as processes and threads, scheduling, synchronization, memory management, file systems, input and output device management and security. The lab implements concepts learned using Linux (Ubuntu) operating systems, providing hands-on experience with installation, shell commands and scripting. Students also practice basic operating concepts such as process and I/O system calls and process scheduling using C programming language.
learning_outcomes: Students will describe the fundamental concepts of process management, memory management, storage management and inter-process communication in operating systems. Students will explain storage management, including mass-storage, file system and I/O system. Students will discuss protection and security. Students will compare across different operating systems environments. Students will implement hands-on expertise on Linux operating system. Students will communicate concepts and techniques in oral presentations. Students will work both independently and collaboratively.

title: Software Engineering 2
description: Software engineering is a discipline that concerns the software development process, focusing on the common principles for creating and maintaining the quality of software products. The core of software engineering is the property of a lifecycle model which covers distinct phases of the software development process, including requirement analysis, software design, implementation, testing and validation, evolution and maintenance.
learning_outcomes: Students will describe software engineering concepts, objectives and software types. Students will describe different software engineering processes. Students will explain software requirements engineering and its methods. Students will recognize different methods for modeling, designing, and testing software. Students will work both independently and collaboratively. Students will communicate concepts and techniques in oral presentations.

title: Human Computer Interaction
description: This course introduces the field of human-computer interaction (HCI), an interdisciplinary field that integrates cognitive psychology, design, computer science and others. Examining the human factors associated with computer information systems provides students with knowledge to understand what influences usability and acceptance of computer systems. The course examines human performance, components of technology, methods and techniques used in design and evaluation of computer systems. Topics covered include interface design, usability evaluation, universal design, multimodal interfaces, virtual reality, and spatial displays. Students work on individual and team assignments to design, implement, and evaluate various interactive systems and user interfaces.
learning_outcomes: Students will describe all aspects of Human-Computer Interaction. Students will explain how humans interact with technology. Students will apply different models of user interface design at various environments. Students will analyze the effects of emotion on user experience. Students will implement windowing systems, applications, architectures, and frameworks. Students will evaluate user interface designs for usability, reliability and accessibility using numbers and statistics. Students will communicate concepts and techniques in oral presentations. Students will work both independently and collaboratively.

title: Artificial Intelligence
description: This course provides students with a comprehensive introduction to the principles, techniques, and applications of Artificial Intelligence. It introduces students to AI with emphasis on solving real world problems for which solutions are difficult to express using traditional algorithmic approaches. Topics covered include intelligent agents, heuristic search techniques, knowledge representation, probabilistic reasoning, machine learning, neural networks, genetic algorithms, fuzzy logic reasoning, self-organizing systems, swarm computing, and biologically inspired computing. The lab educates students in modern intelligent systems concepts including machine learning, intelligent agents, natural language processing, reinforcement learning, and deep learning with convolutional neural networks using Python.
learning_outcomes: Students will describe the intelligent systems fundamental principles. Students will describe knowledge representation techniques. Students will understand neural networks, genetic algorithms, fuzzy logic, swarm computing, biologically inspired computing, expert systems, and machine learning techniques. Students will compare intelligent systems techniques and deployments. Students will evaluate intelligent systems techniques. Students will explore current trends and applications related to intelligent systems. Students will interact in groups collaboratively. Students will communicate concepts and techniques in oral presentations.

title: Design and Analysis of Algorithms
description: This course covers the design and analysis of algorithms, focusing on fundamental algorithmic problem-solving approaches and their efficiency. Topics include fundamentals of algorithm efficiency analysis, brute force, divide and conquer, decrease and conquer, transform and conquer, space and time trade-offs, dynamic programming, greedy techniques, iterative improvement, and limitations of algorithmic power. Students develop the ability to analyze problems, design efficient algorithms, and understand the relationship between data structures and algorithms.
learning_outcomes: Students will define the concept of algorithms. Students will understand the relationship between data structures and algorithms. Students will understand approaches to algorithmic problem solving including brute force, divide and conquer, decrease and conquer, transform and conquer, dynamic programming, and greedy techniques. Students will understand and compute the complexity of algorithms. Students will define and analyze a problem and plan strategies for algorithm development. Students will design algorithms for solving problems. Students will design algorithms efficiently and accurately. Students will work both independently and collaboratively.

title: Theory of Computation
description: This course introduces the theory of computation through a set of abstract machines that serve as models for computation — finite automata, pushdown automata, and Turing machines — and examines the relationship between these automata and formal languages. Topics include deterministic and nondeterministic machines, regular expressions, context-free grammar, regular languages, context-free languages, the Church-Turing thesis, decidability, reducibility, advanced topics in computability, time complexity, NP-completeness, and NP-complete problems.
learning_outcomes: Students will discuss key notions of computation such as algorithm, computability, decidability, reducibility, and complexity through problem solving. Students will explain the models of computation including formal languages, grammars and automata, and their connections. Students will state and explain the Church-Turing thesis and its significance. Students will analyze and design finite automata, pushdown automata, Turing machines, formal languages, and grammars. Students will solve computational problems regarding their computability and complexity and prove the basic results of the theory of computation. Students will initiate groups collaboratively.

title: Ethics in Computing
description: This course explores the ethical considerations and challenges associated with computing, information systems, and emerging technologies. Topics include privacy, security, intellectual property, access and equity, social responsibility, and the impact of computing on society, the environment, and human well-being. The course introduces students to ethical theories and frameworks that guide ethical decision-making in computing, and includes a review of related laws and regulations including the Saudi Act for Anti-Cyber Crime Law. Through case studies and group discussions, students apply ethical principles to real-world scenarios.
learning_outcomes: Students will understand the fundamental ethical principles and frameworks that guide ethical decision-making in computing. Students will understand the social, cultural, and economic implications of computing, information systems, and emerging technologies on society and the environment. Students will understand the legal and regulatory frameworks governing computing and information systems and their implications for ethical practices. Students will analyze and evaluate ethical issues related to computing using critical thinking and ethical reasoning skills. Students will apply ethical principles and frameworks to real-world scenarios in computing and information systems. Students will demonstrate effective communication skills through group discussions and case studies related to ethical issues in computing. Students will develop and implement strategies to promote ethical practices in computing. Students will demonstrate a commitment to upholding ethical principles and values in computing. Students will demonstrate responsibility and accountability for ethical decision-making in computing. Students will develop a personal code of ethics reflecting a commitment to social responsibility. Students will develop an understanding of the role of computing professionals in promoting ethical practices and social responsibility.

title: Mobile Application Development
description: This course introduces students to mobile computing and mobile application development from three perspectives: mobile technology, application development, and user interaction. Topics include mobile computing applications, technologies, wireless communication, common paradigms in mobile computing, user interface and user experience design, and application development guidelines. Students use mobile application frameworks and development environments including Android IDEs and APIs, and learn to build Android applications with components such as activities, services, broadcast receivers, and content providers. The lab covers interface and layout, event handling, application components, data persistence with SQLite, and interaction with mobile device capabilities including location services, messaging, sensors, and Firebase.
learning_outcomes: Students will describe mobile computing systems and applications including native, hybrid, and web apps. Students will analyze and design Activity layouts, views, view groups, and widgets for building user interfaces. Students will list and describe event handling in UI with both XML and Java code solutions. Students will explain the use of Android IDEs and APIs effectively to implement, test, and debug Android applications. Students will build Android applications with application components including activities, services, broadcast receivers, and content providers. Students will develop customized applications with SQLite and handle communication and web services. Students will work both independently and collaboratively.

title: Theory of Compilers
description: This course covers the theory and design of compilers using modern concepts. Students learn the basic elements of a language translator including lexical analysis, parsing, code generation, symbol table management, type checking, scope resolution, code optimization, and error recovery. Students learn to write regular expressions and context-free grammars and understand the separate phases of compilation. A semester-long incremental design project is employed. The lab provides practical experiments on compilers and familiarity with compiled codes in assembly language, including writing scanners, predictive parsers, and performing experiments with scanner and parser generators.
learning_outcomes: Students will describe the components of how a compiler works. Students will describe parsing theory and grammar implementation. Students will implement a simple working compiler. Students will explain the structure of compilers using a novice software compiler. Students will develop compiler construction components such as lexical analysis, top-down and bottom-up parsing, context-sensitive analysis, and intermediate code generation. Students will demonstrate the basic data structures used in compiler construction such as abstract syntax trees, symbol tables, three-address code, and stack machines. Students will interact in groups collaboratively. Students will communicate concepts and techniques in oral presentations.

title: Field Training
description: This course provides students with practical field training experience in real CS work environments over 8 weeks (320 hours). Students are placed in technology organizations where they participate actively in workplace activities under the supervision of both an academic supervisor and a field supervisor. The training bridges theoretical knowledge gained in the program with real-world IT professional practice, exposing students to organizational structures, IT roles and responsibilities, and technology-based problem solving in actual work settings.
learning_outcomes: Students will define terminologies used in CS work environments. Students will describe the organizational structure of an institution and the activities done in a real work environment. Students will identify the roles and responsibilities of IT workers such as IT engineers, programmers, and technicians. Students will analyze the IT requirements of an institution and the existing solutions and tools. Students will apply technology-based solutions to solve real-life problems. Students will communicate IT problems, ideas, and findings with IT professionals. Students will exercise work ethics related to the information technology field in the workplace. Students will work effectively in a team while taking responsibilities.

title: Computer Graphics
description: This course introduces fundamental concepts of computer graphics, providing an overview of computer graphics hardware, algorithms, and software. Topics include 2D and 3D object representation, geometric transformations, 2D and 3D viewing, illumination models, light and color, and color applications. The lab covers raster scan graphics including line and circle drawing, polygon filling, anti-aliasing techniques, clipping, hidden-line and hidden surface methods, ray tracing, and rendering. Both 2D and 3D transformations for the representation of graphic systems are also included, with lab implementation carried out using Java programming.
learning_outcomes: Students will recognize fundamental principles of computer graphics. Students will describe basic techniques for operating with two- and three-dimensional objects. Students will develop key algorithms for modeling and rendering graphical shapes. Students will develop computer graphics-based applications. Students will work both independently and collaboratively. Students will communicate concepts and techniques in oral presentations.

title: Research Methods
description: This course introduces students to the analysis of literature in selected areas of research in computer science. Students learn how to read and evaluate technical papers, select research topics, devise research questions, plan research, and analyze experimental results. At the end of the course, students learn and practice how to conduct an independent research topic and write a research proposal.
learning_outcomes: Students will define research and its fundamental concepts and explain components of a research proposal. Students will select a research design appropriate to a research topic. Students will distinguish between a variety of research methods and designs including quantitative, qualitative, and mixed methods. Students will read and critically review the literature of a particular research topic. Students will discuss primary characteristics of data analysis. Students will discuss findings with reference to research objectives, questions, hypotheses, and relevant literature. Students will observe research ethics such as avoiding plagiarism, acknowledging sources, and using data for research purposes only.

title: Introduction to Data Mining
description: This course focuses on pattern discovery, covering why pattern discovery is important, major techniques for efficient pattern mining, and how to apply pattern discovery in real-world applications. It also covers cluster analysis concepts and methodologies, including partitioning methods such as k-means, hierarchical methods such as BIRCH, density-based methods such as DBSCAN, and grid-based methods such as CLIQUE. Additionally, the course discusses core data mining algorithms including clustering, classification, association rules, and prediction.
learning_outcomes: Students will describe the pattern discovery overview and major issues in data mining. Students will explain pattern evaluation and mining of diverse frequent patterns. Students will describe core data mining algorithms including clustering, classification, association rules, and prediction. Students will understand data preprocessing techniques including data cleaning, integration, reduction, transformation, and discretization. Students will analyze data objects, attribute types, basic statistical descriptions, and data similarity and dissimilarity measures.

title: Senior Project for CS 1
description: This course is the first part of the capstone course for the CS program, representing a real-life experience where students team up to solve a real-world computer science problem by applying knowledge acquired across various undergraduate courses. The course equips students with basic skills to conduct research in computer science, provides guidance in selecting projects, understanding the research process, and using tools needed to implement systems and write documentation. Topics include research methods such as experiments, surveys, interviews, and simulations, as well as literature review, requirements analysis, system analysis and design, and visual presentation preparation. Students investigate, analyze, and design a solution for a studied problem, communicating their achievements through a project report, oral presentation, and poster.
learning_outcomes: Students will define a real-life problem and describe it from inception to a CS solution plan. Students will create a requirements analysis for a real-life problem in its problem environment. Students will analyze the required tasks to solve an outlined problem. Students will explain a preliminary version of the proposed solution. Students will write a comprehensive project report. Students will demonstrate the ability to communicate with and learn from experts across different domains. Students will present concepts and techniques through oral presentations.

title: Modeling and Simulation
description: This course introduces computer simulation technologies and techniques for data modeling. It provides foundations for students to understand computer simulation needs, and to implement and test a variety of simulation and data analysis libraries and programs. The course focuses on building simulation software environments rather than simply using pre-existing packages, and introduces concepts for modeling layers of critical infrastructure networks. Topics include discrete-event simulation, simulation software, statistical models, queuing models, random-number and random-variate generation, input modeling, output analysis, and verification, calibration, and validation of simulation models.
learning_outcomes: Students will describe the role of important elements of discrete event simulation and the modeling paradigm. Students will conceptualize real-world situations related to systems development decisions originating from source requirements and goals. Students will interpret simulation models and apply results to resolve critical issues in real-world environments. Students will apply random numbers and variates to develop simulation models. Students will analyze output data produced by a model and test the validity of the model. Students will explain the concepts of verification and validation of simulation models. Students will demonstrate the ability to work independently and collaboratively and meet deadlines on assignments and projects.

title: Parallel and Distributed Computing
description: This course delivers fundamental issues and recent trends in distributed and parallel computing. It demonstrates the complexities of distributed computing systems and their solutions. Topics include architectural styles, process communication, synchronization, consistency, replication, and fault tolerance techniques in parallel and distributed systems. The course also covers emerging technologies in distributed systems such as blockchain and MapReduce.
learning_outcomes: Students will describe the complexities of parallel and distributed computing systems and approaches to solve those complexities. Students will distinguish the goals and architectures of parallel and distributed computing. Students will discuss important issues in modern parallel and distributed computing systems. Students will recognize different fault tolerance techniques. Students will explore current trends in parallel and distributed computing systems. Students will use communication concepts and techniques in oral presentations. Students will demonstrate the ability to work independently and collaboratively.

title: Senior Project for CS 2
description: This course is the second part of the capstone sequence for the Computer Science program, serving as the culmination of study towards the Bachelor of Computer Science degree. Building on Senior Project 1, this course focuses on implementing, documenting, and orally presenting the results of the project. Students recall requirements analysis results, recognize and apply an appropriate design approach, develop system components, perform system integration and testing, and produce a final project report and oral presentation.
learning_outcomes: Students will recall the results of requirements analysis from the prior project phase. Students will describe the design approach appropriate for the problem. Students will analyze a problem solution. Students will develop the system components. Students will evaluate the developed system. Students will write a comprehensive project report. Students will demonstrate the ability to communicate with and learn from experts from different domains. Students will evaluate and collaborate effectively in group settings. Students will present concepts and techniques through oral presentations.

title: Emerging Technologies
description: This course introduces students to new and innovative technologies and evaluates how modern technologies have fundamentally reshaped organizations and society. It examines how modern organizations operate, produce products, and communicate internally and with external partners using online collaborative technologies developed in the context of social networking and online communities. Topics include how corporations are reengineering business processes, how advances in telecommunications have changed the scope of business, the impact of social media on business practice and society, contemporary technological dilemmas in areas such as climate change, biology, and information technology, and governance and globalization in the context of emerging innovations.
learning_outcomes: Students will describe how information systems have affected the environment for organizations. Students will describe how modern organizations operate in a global environment. Students will explain how advances in telecommunications technologies have changed the scope and pace of business. Students will explain how social media has affected business practice. Students will recognize how social media and pervasive computing have affected how business works. Students will describe how the knowledge-based economy has affected international relationships. Students will demonstrate the ability to work both independently and collaboratively.

title: Unix OS Environment
description: This course introduces the fundamental concepts, principles, and abstractions underlying the design and architecture of Unix systems. Students learn how a Unix system works from the hardware level up to the application level. The course focuses on developing command of the Unix shell environment through understanding of Unix commands, utilities, and networking capabilities, as well as the fundamentals of systems programming in Unix. Topics include basic Linux commands, file system hierarchy, file processing and manipulation, redirection and pattern matching, shell scripting, sed and awk, processes and programming, and remote connectivity.
learning_outcomes: Students will describe the UNIX system and how it works. Students will describe and use the UNIX file system, filters and pattern matching, shell programming, awk programming, processes, and system administration. Students will discuss the current use of UNIX and its advantages. Students will compare the Unix system with other similar operating systems. Students will manipulate files using a text editor from the command line. Students will demonstrate the ability to work both independently and collaboratively.

title: Advanced Programming and Design Patterns
description: This course provides students with advanced programming practices including database programming and multithreading. It introduces design patterns as general repeatable solutions to commonly occurring problems in software design. Topics include advanced programming concepts and techniques, database programming, multithreading, and the three major categories of design patterns: creational, structural, and behavioral patterns.
learning_outcomes: Students will recall the methods and functions used in database programming. Students will define the principles and benefits of design patterns. Students will describe how design patterns can be applied to solve software design problems. Students will develop software applications using advanced programming techniques. Students will analyze real-world problems that can be designed following a design pattern technique. Students will code programs that adopt a design pattern. Students will demonstrate the ability to work both independently and collaboratively.

title: Blockchain Technology
description: This course provides an in-depth understanding of blockchain technology and its practical applications. It covers the fundamentals of blockchain, its architecture, consensus algorithms, smart contracts, cryptography, and decentralized applications. Students learn how to implement blockchain solutions and develop decentralized applications. The course also addresses the potential applications of blockchain in various industries, cryptocurrencies, challenges and limitations of the technology, the legal and regulatory framework around blockchain, and the future impact of blockchain on society and businesses.
learning_outcomes: Students will demonstrate a comprehensive understanding of the fundamental concepts and principles of blockchain technology. Students will explain the architecture of blockchain, its components, and the different consensus algorithms used in blockchain. Students will analyze the potential applications of blockchain in various industries and the challenges faced in implementing the technology. Students will develop decentralized applications using blockchain technology. Students will apply cryptography techniques to secure blockchain systems and transactions. Students will appreciate the ethical implications of blockchain technology and its impact on society. Students will demonstrate autonomy and responsibility in evaluating the legal and regulatory framework around blockchain technology.

title: Digital Marketing
description: This course provides students with knowledge about the business advantages of digital marketing and its importance for marketing success. Topics include developing a digital marketing plan, conducting SWOT analysis, defining target groups, exploring various digital channels and their integration, creating marketing content, website and SEO optimization, Google AdWords campaigns, social media planning, web analytics using Google Analytics, customer relationship management, digital marketing budgeting, and future trends in digital marketing.
learning_outcomes: Students will recognize digital marketing types and their role in marketing success. Students will define digital marketing and its fundamental concepts. Students will identify the main digital marketing models. Students will explain how to manage customer relationships across all digital channels and build better customer relationships. Students will create a digital marketing plan starting from SWOT analysis and defining a target group. Students will analyze digital channels, their advantages and limitations. Students will interact in groups collaboratively.

title: Digital Entrepreneurship
description: This course provides students with knowledge concepts of digital entrepreneurship and enables them to learn the steps and stages of establishing a pilot project. It covers the concept and importance of digital entrepreneurship, establishing entrepreneurial projects, business planning and financing, operational planning, management and marketing, operations management, quality control, challenges of digital entrepreneurial projects, and leadership in digital entrepreneurship. The course includes a practical component where students apply an executive action plan on a chosen project.
learning_outcomes: Students will list the needs and challenges of digital entrepreneurial projects. Students will explain the strategies and mechanisms for the success of digital entrepreneurial projects. Students will diagnose problems related to digital entrepreneurial projects and present proposed solutions. Students will compare the characteristics of digital entrepreneurial projects with traditional small projects. Students will participate in the design and implementation of a business plan for an entrepreneurial project model. Students will demonstrate effective communication with entities supporting digital entrepreneurs such as leadership centers and business incubators.

title: Software Testing
description: This course is designed to equip bachelor degree students with the necessary skills to get familiar with software testing. Software testing is defined as work to check whether the actual results match the expected outcome and to make sure that the software is problem-free. It requires the execution of a software component or system component to evaluate one or more interest properties. Software testing also helps identify gaps, missing requirements, and errors contrary to the actual requirements. Topics include foundations of testing, testing throughout the software life cycle, test design techniques, test organization, and tool support for testing.
learning_outcomes: Students will apply software testing knowledge and engineering methods. Students will design and conduct a software test process for a software testing project. Students will identify the needs of software test automation and define and develop a test tool to support test automation. Students will understand and identify various software testing problems and solve these problems by designing and selecting software test models, criteria, strategies, and methods. Students will use various communication methods and skills to communicate with teammates to conduct practice-oriented software testing projects. Students will demonstrate basic understanding and knowledge of contemporary issues in software testing such as component-based software testing problems. Students will use software testing methods and modern software testing tools for their testing projects. Students will gain techniques and skills on how to use modern software testing tools to support software testing projects.

title: Machine Learning
description: This course provides an overview of Machine Learning (ML). ML is a key to develop intelligent systems and analyze data in science and engineering. ML engines enable intelligent technologies such as virtual assistants and self-driving cars. This course provides an introduction to the fundamental methods at the core of modern ML, covering theoretical foundations as well as essential algorithms for supervised and unsupervised learning. Lab sessions demonstrate ML algorithms in practice including K-Nearest Neighbors, K-Means, SVM, and Naive Bayes Classifier, covering topics such as introduction to AI and ML, math refresher, data pre-processing, regression, supervised learning classification, unsupervised learning clustering, semi-supervised learning, reinforcement learning, and deep learning.
learning_outcomes: Students will describe the fundamentals of machine learning and the main research activities in this field. Students will recognize suitable algorithms to tackle different machine learning problems. Students will illustrate the strengths and weaknesses of multiple machine learning approaches. Students will compare existing ML algorithms for selecting suitable model parameters. Students will build models for prediction and data organization from data. Students will develop various machine learning algorithms in a range of real-world applications. Students will employ group collaboration effectively. Students will explain concepts and techniques in oral presentations. Students will react both independently and collaboratively.

title: Network Switching and Routing
description: This course is for students who elect to go into the networking track of the computer science program. Students build on their basic understanding of computer networks to develop extended theoretical and practical knowledge of advanced routing. The course provides coverage of methods used for routing traffic across both large and small network infrastructures, ranging from small scale static routing to the use of dynamic routing protocols such as EGP, BGP, OSPF and EIGRP. Lab sessions provide practical experience using Packet Tracer network simulator, covering basic routing device configuration, setup and troubleshooting for WANs and enterprise networks, as well as configuration, verification and troubleshooting of static routing, RIP, BGP, OSPF and EIGRP routing protocols.
learning_outcomes: Students will define core switching concepts at the network layer. Students will define core concepts of routing protocols of static and dynamic routing. Students will define the basic architecture of RIP, BGP, OSPF and EIGRP routing protocols. Students will apply different mathematical and logical principles related to IPv4/IPv6 addressing and subnetting basics. Students will perform basic routing devices and basic routing configuration and troubleshooting techniques for WANs and enterprise networks. Students will perform dynamic routing protocols configuration, verification and troubleshooting for RIP, BGP, OSPF and EIGRP routing protocols. Students will work both independently and collaboratively.

title: Systems Programming
description: This course provides an introductory overview of operating systems and system programming, mainly focusing on system-level programming based on OS services and other APIs. Topics include system calls, file I/O, files and directories, memory management, process control, inter-process communication (IPC), socket-based network programming, remote procedure call (RPC) programming, and basic security mechanisms. Lab topics cover basic Unix commands, memory management, event driven programming, process management and CPU scheduling, servers and sockets, and threads and synchronization. Course work includes substantial programming homework and team-based projects using C, C++, Java, and Perl.
learning_outcomes: Students will understand the internal operation of Linux systems such as processes, programs, process groups, signals, address space, user and kernel modes, system calls, and context switching. Students will produce medium complexity shell scripts to automate repetitive tasks. Students will write simple C programs similar to standard Linux utilities using Linux system calls. Students will identify the central role of concurrency in systems programming and produce programs which generate, link and control multiple processes. Students will write sophisticated programs by making efficient use of OS services and reusing existing system utilities. Students will communicate concepts and techniques in oral presentations. Students will work both independently and collaboratively.

title: Database 2
description: This course introduces the fundamental concepts and principles of database management systems (DBMS), covering basic terminology and ideas of relational databases. Students gain knowledge about triggers and transactions in PL/SQL and SQL, including complex objects such as views, sequences, indexes and synonyms, PL/SQL block structure, variable declarations, program control structures, cursors and parameters, functions, procedures, packages, exceptions, and triggers. The second part of the course covers the concepts and fundamental principles of NoSQL databases, including the four main database types: Key-Value Stores, Columnar Databases, Document Stores, and Graph Databases.
learning_outcomes: Students will identify complex objects including views, sequences, indexes and synonyms. Students will demonstrate the main concepts of PL/SQL programming. Students will manipulate complex objects like views, sequences, indexes and synonyms. Students will use PL/SQL programming with DBMS. Students will execute functions, procedures, packages, exceptions and triggers. Students will execute the different types of NoSQL databases. Students will work both independently and collaboratively.

title: Wireless Networks
description: This course provides an introduction to various current and next generation wireless networking technologies, undertaking a detailed exploration of fundamental architectural and design principles used at all layers. Related protocols and their performance are studied using formal analytical tools and realistic simulations. Topics include wireless network architectures such as cellular networks, WLANs, and multi-hop networks; radio propagation models; digital modulation and coding under wireless fading environments; CDMA and OFDM; diversity and MIMO; randomized medium access including Aloha and CSMA; channel allocation; multi-hop wireless network routing protocols; geographic routing; backpressure routing; TCP over wireless networks; and congestion control mechanisms.
learning_outcomes: Students will understand the architecture and applications of current and next generation wireless networks including cellular, WLANs, sensor networks, mobile ad-hoc networks and intermittently connected mobile networks. Students will understand key concepts and techniques underlying modern physical layer wireless communications including radio propagation modeling, digital modulation, CDMA, OFDM, diversity and MIMO. Students will learn how to design and analyze various medium access and resource allocation techniques. Students will design and analyze network layer routing protocols including proactive, reactive, opportunistic, and backpressure routing. Students will design and analyze transport layer protocols with emphasis on congestion control including TCP over wireless. Students will evaluate MAC and network protocols using network simulation software tools. Students will work both independently and collaboratively.

title: Natural Language Processing
description: This course introduces fundamental techniques in the field of natural language processing (NLP). It covers methods for parsing, knowledge representations, corpus-based methods, and information retrieval topics related to NLP. Popular NLP algorithms such as CFGs, CYK, N-grams, vector space model, and HMM are taught, along with evaluation of NLP tasks using precision and recall. Lab sessions use Python with the Natural Language Toolkit (NLTK) for practical computational linguistics experiments, covering accessing text corpora and lexical resources, processing raw text, categorizing and tagging words, extracting information from text, classifying text, and analyzing sentence structure. Topics include text processing, language models, POS tagging, context-free grammars, probabilistic context-free grammars, parsing, lexical semantics, information retrieval, machine translation, and question answering.
learning_outcomes: Students will define and contrast deterministic and stochastic grammars providing examples to show the adequacy of each. Students will identify the challenges of language models and represent text meaning. Students will realize the advantages of standard corpora by using variety of NLP tasks such as Part of Speech tagging, parsing, and various normalization tasks. Students will identify techniques for information retrieval, language translation, and text classification. Students will simulate, apply, or implement classic and stochastic algorithms for parsing natural language. Students will practice techniques for information retrieval, language translation, and text classification. Students will communicate concepts and techniques in oral presentations. Students will adapt to work both independently and collaboratively.

title: Digital Image Processing and Analysis
description: This course introduces basic mathematical concepts, algorithms, and building elements in digital image processing and analysis. Topics include image acquisition and sensing, restoration and enhancement in the spatial and frequency domains such as noise reduction and de-blurring, color image processing, various image compression techniques, morphological image processing, image edge detection, region segmentation, object recognition and watersheds, and image representation and description. Lab experiments use the MATLAB image processing toolbox, covering intensity transformations, fuzzy image processing, spatial and frequency domain filtering, image restoration and reconstruction, geometric transformations, color image processing, wavelets, image compression, morphological image processing, image segmentation, feature extraction, and image pattern classification.
learning_outcomes: Students will understand the definition and concept of digital image processing. Students will recognize the digital image fundamentals. Students will apply various techniques for intensity transformations, spatial filtering, function reconstruction, image restoration, reconstruction, and aliasing. Students will implement spatial filtering on color images, wavelet packet representations, image compression, and watermarking. Students will perform tasks such as morphological smoothing, edge detection, and extracting connected components. Students will communicate concepts and techniques in oral presentations. Students will work both independently and collaboratively.

title: Cross-Platform Development
description: This course covers cross-platform application development using React and React Native. Topics include an overview of cross-platform development, introduction to React, React components and their lifecycle, state and props management, React hooks, responsive user interface design with CSS Grid and Flexbox, handling user input and events, client-side routing with React Router, consuming RESTful APIs with Axios, state management with Redux, advanced React concepts including Higher-Order Components, Render Props and the Context API, and cross-platform mobile development with React Native including navigation, custom components, and animations.
learning_outcomes: Students will discuss the basics of cross-platform development and its importance. Students will explain the concepts of components, state management, routing, and API integration. Students will explain the principles of responsive design and how to apply them in cross-platform development. Students will develop cross-platform applications. Students will build responsive and interactive user interfaces using components. Students will manage state in applications. Students will implement routing in cross-platform applications. Students will integrate APIs into applications. Students will work both independently and collaboratively.

title: Network Design
description: This course prepares students to design and manage various aspects of organizational networks. Topics include business goals and constraints analysis, top-down network design methodology, technical goals and tradeoffs analysis, existing internetwork characterization, network topology design, addressing and numbering model, routing protocols selection, network security strategies design, network management strategies, technologies and devices for enterprise networks, and network design testing and optimization. Lab sessions cover logical and physical LAN design, measuring LAN design performance, logical and physical Campus Network/WAN design, and measuring Campus Network/WAN design performance using network simulator software such as Packet Tracer.
learning_outcomes: Students will outline the network design process and internetworking characteristics. Students will describe technologies and devices for enterprise networks. Students will design different network topologies and physical networks. Students will apply network management strategies. Students will analyze business goals and constraints using a top-down network design methodology. Students will apply addressing and numbering models, routing protocols, security strategies, and testing and optimization techniques. Students will work both independently and collaboratively.

title: Recommender Systems
description: This course provides students with the fundamentals of recommender systems, covering collaborative recommendation (CR), content-based recommendation (CB), knowledge-based recommendation, and hybrid recommendation approaches. Students learn how to develop recommender system applications and how they integrate into websites. Additional topics include explanations in recommender systems and attacks on collaborative recommender systems. Lab sessions provide hands-on experience implementing recommender system techniques.
learning_outcomes: Students will describe principles of recommender systems. Students will memorize various recommender algorithms and approaches. Students will explain how to develop recommender systems. Students will demonstrate when and how to apply recommender systems techniques. Students will communicate concepts and techniques in oral presentations. Students will work both independently and collaboratively.

title: Data Science Fundamentals
description: This course provides students with a comprehensive understanding of the fundamental concepts, tools, and techniques used in data science. Students learn the essentials of Python programming, data manipulation, data visualization, statistical analysis, machine learning, and deep learning, equipping them with skills to tackle real-world data-driven problems. Topics include the data science process, data manipulation and cleaning, exploratory data analysis, introduction to machine learning, regression techniques, classification techniques, unsupervised learning, and deep learning fundamentals. Lab sessions use Python libraries such as Pandas, NumPy, Matplotlib, Seaborn, and Scikit-learn, covering data manipulation with Pandas, exploratory data analysis and visualization, regression and classification modeling, unsupervised learning, and deep learning with TensorFlow or Keras. Students also develop data communication skills to present findings to technical and non-technical audiences.
learning_outcomes: Students will understand the key concepts and principles of data science including data collection, preprocessing, analysis, and visualization. Students will identify various machine learning techniques such as supervised learning and unsupervised learning and their applications in data science. Students will use programming languages such as Python or R and related libraries to preprocess, analyze, and visualize data. Students will develop and implement machine learning models for tasks such as classification, regression, clustering, and recommendation systems. Students will evaluate the performance of machine learning models using appropriate metrics and validation techniques. Students will work both independently and collaboratively.

title: Computer Games Programming
description: This course demonstrates an understanding of computer game programming. Concepts are applied in the design and implementation of computer games. Students implement a non-trivial computer game using industry-standard techniques. Topics include an introduction to the programming language and environment, the building blocks of programming, variables, types and methods, control flow and collection types, game design primer, building a level, lighting basics, animating, movement, camera controls and collisions, and scripting game mechanics. Lab sessions use Unity and C# covering environment setup, programming fundamentals, game design principles, movement and camera systems, and scripting game mechanics. Students also develop collaborative and oral communication skills through group projects and presentations.
learning_outcomes: Students will describe programming aspects of computer games. Students will describe the history of computer and video game technology, game genres and design principles, and the social impact of games. Students will design games using a commonly used game environment. Students will demonstrate tools and techniques for programming interactive games and virtual reality simulations. Students will solve problems by applying different mathematical, logical, and physical principles. Students will interact in groups collaboratively. Students will communicate concepts and techniques in oral presentations.

title: Network Administration
description: This course focuses on the design, installation, configuration, and operation of local area networks. It provides students with the knowledge and skills necessary to install and configure stand-alone and client computers that are part of a workgroup or domain. Students explore topics in network administration in both theoretical and practical ways, studying different software platforms, shared resources, administration, security, and anti-virus procedures and methodologies. Lab sessions provide practical experiments on managing a network, covering performance measurement using utility software, defending the network by configuring firewalls, anti-virus, and proxy servers, managing users in different operating systems, and backup and disaster recovery procedures. Topics include the role of system administrators, NAT, DHCP, DNS, web and mail servers, administration architecture, network management protocols, security planning, vulnerability assessment, maintenance types and tasks, and backup and disaster recovery.
learning_outcomes: Students will describe the concept of network and system administration. Students will describe the roles of a network and system administrator. Students will describe the SNMP protocol. Students will demonstrate an understanding of hardware and software configuration basics. Students will demonstrate an understanding of the importance of network security policies in Linux and Windows environments. Students will modify, configure, implement, and verify networking services for intranet and internet domains. Students will analyze, assess, and troubleshoot network performance, connectivity, and security issues. Students will communicate concepts and techniques in oral presentations. Students will work both independently and collaboratively.

title: Robotics
description: This course provides an overview of robotic systems. Topics covered include fundamentals of robotics, actuators and drive systems, sensors, systems on chip microcontrollers, control programs, robotic actions and autonomous control algorithms, object detection and classification, neural networks, and machine learning. The course includes an introduction to scientific programming and concludes with student project presentations. Lab and project work emphasize hands-on application of robotics concepts through demonstrations, group projects, and case studies.
learning_outcomes: Students will recognize fundamental principles of robotics. Students will describe actuators, drive systems, and sensors. Students will explain system on a chip microcontrollers. Students will illustrate autonomous control algorithms. Students will illustrate object detection and classification algorithms. Students will explain neural networks and machine learning. Students will communicate concepts and techniques in oral presentations. Students will work both independently and collaboratively.

title: Cloud Computing
description: This course provides a theoretical and practical background in cloud computing fundamental concepts, models, technology mechanisms, and technology architectures for secure cloud-based datacenters. It equips students with solid theoretical knowledge to understand the inner mechanics, architectural layers, and models of cloud computing. Topics include introduction to cloud computing, fundamental concepts and models, cloud-enabling technology, fundamental cloud security, cloud security mechanisms, cloud infrastructure mechanisms, fundamental cloud architecture, cloud delivery model considerations, cost metrics and pricing models, and service quality metrics and SLAs. Lab sessions use the CloudSim simulation framework and cover Linux OS, GCC compiler, Git repositories, datacenter creation, virtual machines and cloudlets, processor scheduling, and datacenter host resource management.
learning_outcomes: Students will recognize fundamental principles of cloud computing. Students will describe the different types of cloud delivery models. Students will distinguish between different cloud deployment models. Students will analyze cloud management, security, and infrastructure mechanisms. Students will evaluate service quality metrics and SLAs. Students will implement a simulation of large-scale cloud computing data centers. Students will communicate concepts and techniques in oral presentations. Students will work both independently and collaboratively.
